summary: This lesson teaches 'más' as 'else' (nada más = nothing else, nadie más = nobody else), the preterite of 'ver' (irregular), time-related vocabulary with 'vez' (una vez, otra vez, una vez más), and possessive adjectives (mi, tu, nuestro/a, su) including agreement rules.

teachings.summary: Adding 'más' to negative words like nada or nadie gives the meaning of 'else': nada más (nothing else), nadie más (nobody else). 'Una vez más' means 'one more time'.
teachings.rule: nada más = nothing else / anything else; nadie más = nobody else / anybody else

examples.english: I want to see them one more time.

teachings.summary: Ver is irregular in the preterite: vi, vio, vimos, viste, vieron. The endings follow the -er/-ir pattern but the stem is just v-.

examples.english: Didn't you see me last night?

teachings.summary: Vez means 'time' in the sense of occasion (not clock time). It is feminine (una vez). 'Otra vez' means 'again' (another time). 'A la vez' means 'at the same time'.

examples.english: I want to see them again.
Did you go out to dance again?

examples.spanish: Todos llegaron a mi casa a la vez.
examples.english: Everybody arrived at my house at the same time.

teachings.notes: Vez is feminine (otra vez, not otro vez). V-e-z.

teachings.summary: Full review of possessive adjectives: mi/mis (my), tu/tus (your informal), nuestro/nuestra/nuestros/nuestras (our), su/sus (his/her/their/your formal). They agree with the thing possessed, not the possessor. 'Propio/a' means 'own'.
teachings.rule: Mi, tu, su change only for number (mi/mis, tu/tus, su/sus). Nuestro changes for both gender and number (nuestro/nuestra/nuestros/nuestras). Su/sus covers his, her, their, and your (formal) -- the plural 'sus' refers to plural things possessed, not plural possessors.

examples.english: I want to go to his/their houses.
We are with our friends. (all female)

examples.spanish: Lo vi con mis propios ojos.
examples.english: I saw it with my own eyes.

teachings.notes: Nuestro contains the o-split visible in nosotros. Avoid saying 'sus' just because you mean 'they' -- sus only for plural possessed items.

teachings.summary: The -ndo form (gerund) is appropriate in structures like 'he arrived wanting to speak with me', where English also uses -ing.

examples.spanish: Llegó a mi casa otra vez queriendo hablar conmigo.
examples.english: He arrived at my house again wanting to speak with me.

teachings.notes: Spanish uses -ing much less than English, but this is one situation where it is used.
